items.q: Which chords form a plagal cadence?
items.why: Students confuse plagal (IV-I) with perfect (V-I) because both end on I. The 'amen' at the end of hymns is the classic plagal cadence - subdominant to tonic, with a softer ecclesiastical feel.

items.q: What does 'piano' (p) mean as a dynamic marking?
items.why: Students see 'piano' and think of the instrument. As dynamic, p = soft (from Italian). The instrument is actually a shortened 'pianoforte' (soft-loud) because it could play at varying dynamics, unlike the harpsichord.

items.q: How many crotchets are in a semibreve?
items.why: Students confuse the relative durations. A semibreve (whole note) = 2 minims = 4 crotchets = 8 quavers. Each level halves in length.

items.q: A dot after a note adds?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: A half of its value. “A quarter of its value” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many quavers equal a crotchet?
items.why: Exact answer: 2 quavers. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
